feedback.text: As a EU citizen, I support the Digital Fairness Act. However, I'd also like to bring your attention to two major issues: 1) Video game publishers often do not have end-of-life plans for their games. This means that once they stop selling and supporting them, no one can ensure that these games will still be available to the consumers that have paid for them. Most of the times, especially in the modern age where a lot of games now need an online service to run, the publishers just outright terminate all access to the said service when the end-of-life date is reached, rendering the game useless. The "Stop Destroying Videogames" EU Citizens initiative (also called "Stop Killing Games") has been created to try to stop this issue, and I strongly believe that it should be considered along with this Act. I think it can make a great impact in the video games industry, helping to prevent unfair and predatory practices by video game publishers. 2) Payment processors like Visa and Mastercard refuse access to digital products and also dictate what products a seller's platform can host or not by forcing the seller(s) to comply. This mostly concerns adult games (see recent Steam and itch.io measures that were taken due to these restrictions), but I can also see it happening to other types of content as well, adult or not. I do not think that payment processors, especially those that are not chosen by the users, should have this much power over online commerce. I think that the ones responsible for what is allowed or not in a seller's platform should be: the seller themselves, the local laws for every country and, most importantly, the community of these platforms. The majority of the community can be trusted to report any unlawful content to the seller, who can then in turn remove it from their platform. Thank you for your time and I hope you will consider mine as well as other citizens' feedback.
